Descriptive Title: Defining Identity (X は Y です)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Establishes the relationship "X is Y" using the topic particle は (pronounced wa) and the copula です. Used to state names, nationalities, and occupations.
Converting statements into questions by adding the particle か at the end. Includes the use of question words like 何時 (what time) and 何歳 (how old).
Using the particle の to link nouns. It indicates possession (e.g., "my teacher") or categorization/restriction (e.g., "Japanese language student").

Descriptive Title: Demonstrative Pronouns (これ・それ・あれ)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Using これ, それ, and あれ to refer to objects based on their proximity to the speaker and listener. どれ is the question word for "which one."

Descriptive Title: Specifying Objects (この・その・あの)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Demonstrative determiners that must be followed by a noun (e.g., この本 — this book).

Descriptive Title: Identifying Places (ここ・そこ・あそこ)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Words used to identify locations relative to the speaker/listener. どこ is used to ask "where."
Combining the question word だれ (who) with the particle の to ask "whose."
Using も to mean "also" or "too," replacing the particles は or が.
The formal negative form of です, used to state that something is NOT the case.

Descriptive Title: Social Feedback (Particles ね and よ)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Sentence-ending particles: ね for seeking agreement ("right?") and よ for providing new information ("I tell you").
Categorizing verbs into う-verbs, る-verbs, and irregular verbs (くる, する). Teaches the formal ～ます forms for present affirmative and negative.
The present tense is used for habitual actions (e.g., "I often watch TV") or future intent (e.g., "I will go tomorrow").

Descriptive Title: Sentence Logistics (Particles を, で, に, へ)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Particles marking direct objects (を), locations of action (で), and goals/destinations of movement (に or へ).
Using the particle に with specific time expressions like "at 3:00" or "on Sunday."
Formulating "Why don't we...?" suggestions by using the negative question form of a verb.
Using words like よく (often), ときどき (sometimes), and ぜんぜん (not at all) to qualify activities.

Descriptive Title: Stating Existence (あります / います)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Using あります for inanimate objects and います for living beings to say "there is" or "I have."
Describing where things are using location nouns (e.g., 上 — above, 下 — under) and the particle の.

Descriptive Title: Past Identity (Past Tense of です)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Using ～でした (affirmative) and ～じゃなかったです (negative) to describe past states.
Conjugating verbs into the past tense using ～ました and ～ませんでした.
Using と to mean "and" (between nouns) or "with" (someone).
Expressing how long an activity lasts using duration words like 一時間 (for one hour).
Present tense of い-adjectives (ending in ～い) and な-adjectives (taking ～な before nouns).
Past tense forms for adjectives, such as changing the final ～い to ～かったです for い-adjectives.
Placing adjectives directly before nouns to describe them (e.g., おもしろい本 — an interesting book).
Expressing likes and dislikes using 好き (like) and 嫌い (dislike) with the particle が.

Descriptive Title: Making Suggestions (～ましょう / ～ましょうか)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Using ～ましょう to say "Let's..." and ～ましょうか to say "Shall I/we...?".
Conjugating verbs into the て-form, which is used to connect sentences or add auxiliary meanings.
Combining the て-form with ください to make a polite request ("Please do...").
Using the て-form to describe two or more activities performed in chronological order.
Saying "You may do..." or asking for permission ("May I...?").
Expressing prohibition or "You must not do...".
Using から to mean "because" or "therefore" to link a reason to an effect.
Using the て-form with いる to describe actions currently in progress.
Using ～ている for actions that resulted in a lasting state (e.g., "to be married" or "to live in").

Descriptive Title: Describing Appearance (X は Y が Adjective)
Detailed Summary and Scope: A structure to describe parts of a whole, such as "Mary has long hair" (メアリーさんは髪が長いです).
Using て-forms of adjectives and nouns to link multiple descriptive phrases into one sentence.
Expressing the reason for going somewhere (e.g., "go to study" — 勉強しに行く) using the verb stem.
Introduction to short forms (dictionary forms) for verbs and adjectives, used in casual settings and grammar patterns.
Rules for dropping particles and using short forms in informal dialogue between friends or family.

Descriptive Title: Sharing Thoughts (Short Form + と思います)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Using short forms to say "I think that...".

Descriptive Title: Reporting Speech (Short Form + と言っていました)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Using short forms to report what someone else said in the past.
Using the negative short form + でください to say "Please don't...".
Turning a verb into a noun using の to say you like "doing" something (e.g., 食べるのが好き — I like eating).
Using が to mark the subject when introducing new information or answering "who/which" questions.
Using 何か for "something" and 何も (with negative verbs) for "nothing."
Conjugating verbs and adjectives into informal past tense (e.g., ～た for verbs).
Using entire sentences (in short form) to modify a noun (e.g., "The person who is eating").

Descriptive Title: Already and Not Yet (もう / まだ)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Using もう + past tense ("already did") and まだ + ～ていません ("have not yet done").

Descriptive Title: Simple Comparison (A のほうが B より)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Stating that item A is more [adjective] than item B.
Saying that something is the "most" or "best" within a specific group.
Using の to replace a noun that has already been mentioned to avoid repetition (e.g., "the red one").
Using short forms + つもり to express one's intention or plan to do something.
Expressing that something "becomes" a certain way (e.g., あつくなる — to get hot).
Conjugating verbs into the ～たい form to say "I want to do...".

Descriptive Title: Listing Representative Actions (～たり... ～たりする)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Listing a few examples of activities out of many using the ～たり form.
Using the past short form + ことがある to say "I have the experience of...".
Using や to connect nouns when the list is not exhaustive (A, B, and others).
Using ～んです at the end of a sentence to provide a reason, explanation, or emphasis.
Using the verb stem or adjective base + すぎる to say something is "too much" or "excessive."
Recommending a course of action ("It's better to..."). Uses past short form for affirmative advice.
A more polite and objective way to say "because" compared to から.

Descriptive Title: Stating Obligation (～なければいけません)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Expressing "must" or "have to" do something.
Using ～でしょうか for polite wondering or asking "I wonder if...?"
Expresses ability or possibility ("can do"). る-verbs change to ～られる; う-verbs change their final sound to the -e row + る (e.g., 行く → 行ける).
Used to list two or more reasons for a situation, often following a predicate in short form. It implies the reasons provided are not the only ones.
Adding ～そうです to adjective bases to express a guess based on visual or intuitive impressions ("looks like"). いい becomes よさそう.
Using the て-form + みる to express "trying something out" or doing something tentatively to see the outcome.
Used when a predicate applies only to a specific noun and not generally, indicating contrast or limitation ("If it's X...").
Framework for describing how often something happens over a period of time: (period) に (frequency) (e.g., three times a week).
Used to say the speaker "wants" something. Typically uses the が particle for the object. To describe someone else's desire, use ～と言っています or ほしがっている.
Expresses that something is a possibility ("might" or "may"). Follows short forms of predicates; だ is dropped for nouns and な-adjectives.

Descriptive Title: Gifts (あげる / くれる / もらう)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Verbs for giving and receiving objects: あげる (give to others), くれる (give to me), and もらう (receive from someone).
A polite way to suggest a course of action ("Why don't you...?"). Follows the past short form of a verb.
Number + も indicates "as many as," while Number + しか + Negative indicates "as few as" or "only".
The casual equivalent of ～ましょう, used to suggest plans or offer help to close friends.

Descriptive Title: Firm Intentions (Volitional + と思っています)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Used to talk about resolutions or decisions already made ("I intend to..."). と思います indicates a decision made on the spot.
Describes an action performed in advance as preparation for something else. Often shortened to ～とく in speech.
Using entire sentences (in short form) to qualify a noun, similar to "The book that I read".

Descriptive Title: Exchange of Favors (～てあげる / ～てくれる / ～てもらう)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Using giving/receiving verbs with the て-form to describe providing or receiving services as favors.

Descriptive Title: Polite Requests (～ていただけませんか)
Detailed Summary and Scope: A very polite way to ask for a favor, following the て-form of a verb.
Used to hope for good luck for others (～といいですね) or for oneself (～といいんですが).
Using 時 (とき) to describe when an event occurs. The clause before 時 ends in the short form (present for upcoming, past for completed).
A polite way to apologize for a specific action performed in the past.

Descriptive Title: Reporting Hearsay (～そうです - I hear)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Used to report information heard from other sources ("I hear that..."). Follows short forms and keeps だ for nouns and な-adjectives.
The casual/spoken equivalent of ～と言っていました or the hearsay ～そうです.
Expresses "if" or "after." Formed by adding ～ら to the past short form of predicates.

Descriptive Title: Lack of Obligation (～なくてもいいです)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Used to state that something is not necessary ("You don't have to...").
Expresses that something "looks like" or "resembles" another thing based on observation. Follows short forms.
前に (before doing X) always follows the present short form. ～てから (after doing X) follows the て-form.
Introduction to verb pairs where one is transitive (acting on something) and the other is intransitive (change occurring to something).
Describes a state resulting from a change (e.g., 開いている — the window is open).
Indicates finishing a task completely or that something regrettable happened inadvertently.
Expresses cause-and-effect: "Whenever A happens, B happens too." A is always in the present short form.
Connects two verbs performed by the same person at the same time. Follows the verb stem (e.g., 聞きながら).
Expresses "I wish I had done..." or "I should have done..." to describe regret over an alternative course of action.
Special verbs or the お + stem + になる pattern used to exalt the actions of others (superiors or strangers).

Descriptive Title: Respectful Advice (お + Stem + ください)
Detailed Summary and Scope: COURTEOUS commands typically used in public announcements or stores.

Descriptive Title: Gratitude for Actions (～てくれてありがとう)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Expresses thanks for a specific favor. In formal situations, use ～てくださいましてありがとうございました.
States "I am glad that such and such was the case".
Used when you believe something is "supposed to be" or "must be" the case based on logic or evidence.
Special verbs (e.g., 申します, 参ります) used to talk modestly about oneself to show deference to the listener.
A pattern used when the speaker performs an action out of respect for someone else (e.g., お会いしました).
Expresses doing one action "without doing" another (e.g., "studied without sleeping").
Including questions within larger sentences (e.g., "I don't know where she lives"). Uses か for question-words and かどうか for yes/no.
Pattern for introducing a person or item by name ("an item called 'Name'").

Descriptive Title: Ease and Difficulty (～やすい / ～にくい)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Adding ～やすい (easy to do) or ～にくい (hard to do) to a verb stem to create an い-adjective.
Used when a "victim" is affected by a "villain's" act, often indicating annoyance or disadvantage.
Characterizes a state brought about on purpose by someone unnamed (e.g., "The reservation has been made").
A 間に B means event B occurs while state A is in progress.
Using する with adjectives to mean "to make something [adjective]" (e.g., 静かにする — to keep quiet).

Descriptive Title: Desiring Action from Others (～てほしい)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Expresses "I want (someone else) to do something." The target is marked with に.
Expresses making or letting someone do something. る-verbs → ～させる; う-verbs → -a row + せる.

Descriptive Title: Favor of Permission (Causative + くれる/あげる)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Combining causative forms with giving verbs to express "letting me do" or "giving the favor of allowing".
A strong instruction or command often used by parents or teachers.
Condition A that guarantees a good or desired result B ("If you do A, then B...").
Connects two facts where the second holds true in spite of expectations ("Despite the fact X, Y").

Descriptive Title: Comparisons and Examples (～のように / ～のような)
Detailed Summary and Scope: Describes action done "like" something else or a noun similar to a provided example.
Expresses being "made to do" something against one's will; often implies reluctance.
Expresses "even if" or "no matter how." Used for both present and past scenarios.
Expresses "decide to do..." based on short form present tense.
Expresses doing something as a regular rule or habit.
Means "until." The event before まで is always present affirmative.
Noun-forming suffix meaning "the way to do X." Replaces verb stem (e.g., 泳ぎ方).
